title: Pig first aid kit and injection technique
subtitle: What to keep on the holding and how to give a subcutaneous or intramuscular injection safely.
excerpt: Having the right medicines available before you need them saves a pig's life. Waiting for a vet visit for a straight antibiotic course that you could have given on the holding loses you 24 critical hours. Here's what belongs in a smallholder pig kit and how to give injections correctly.
sourceNotes: DEFRA/APHA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs. AHDB Pork, pig health management. Vet prescription requirements under the Veterinary Medicines Regulations 2013.

glossaryTerms.definition: Under the skin; the subcutaneous site for injections in pigs is typically behind the ear, where the skin is relatively loose.
Into a muscle; the standard site for intramuscular injections in pigs is the neck muscle behind the ear.
The minimum time that must pass between the last dose of a veterinary medicine and the animal being slaughtered for food or its milk or eggs being used; specified on the product label.

content.text: Giving a pig an injection requires restraint, the correct site, and the right needle size. Pigs that are not restrained move at the moment of injection and bend or break the needle. The neck muscle injection site is the standard for most pig medicines;
subcutaneous
administration behind the ear is used for some products. All veterinary medicines require a prescription from your vet; keep the original prescription and the product datasheet, and observe the
withdrawal period
What to keep in the kit
Needles, 18 gauge, 25 mm length for most adult pigs; 20 gauge, 16 mm for small pigs and weaners
2 ml, 5 ml, and 20 ml syringes
Prescribed long-acting antibiotic (discuss with your vet before lambing season)
Iron injection for piglets, if you're farrowing sows (prescribed)
A prescription pain and fever reducer, available from your vet
Electrolyte powder for rehydrating scouring piglets
A medicine record book, required by law for any prescription medicine used on livestock
Restraining the pig

For weaners up to 30 kg: hold the pig's body between your legs, facing away from you, its back against your knees. For larger pigs: back the pig into a corner and hold it with a snout rope if needed. A pig standing still in a corner with light pressure from a board against one side will tolerate a neck injection without a snout rope in most cases.
Giving the injection
For
intramuscular
(IM) injection: insert the needle at 90° into the neck muscle behind the ear. Pull the plunger back slightly before injecting, if blood appears, withdraw and redirect. Inject slowly and smoothly.
For subcutaneous (SC) injection: tent the loose skin behind the ear with two fingers and insert the needle at 45° into the tent. No blood should appear on the pull-back. Inject and withdraw.
Use a new needle for each pig. Blunt needles cause more tissue damage and more pain.
Write up the treatment in the medicine book straight away: animal ID, medicine name, dose, and withdrawal period.
